data.title: What Recent Stock Swings Mean for S&P Global’s Market Value in 2025
data.description:
Thinking about what to do with S&P Global stock right now? You are definitely not alone. Whether you are weighing a hold, a buy, or just curious, S&P Global remains one of those companies that always seems to be at the heart of market conversations. The company’s recent stock swings say a lot about shifting investor sentiment. Over the past week, the shares have nudged 0.1% higher, but that comes after a bumpy month where the price dropped 11.2%. For the year so far, S&P Global is down 2.5%, and if you zoom out, it is off 8.1% over the last 12 months. Yet, anyone with a long memory will notice that over three years, the stock is up an impressive 66.8%, and it has delivered a solid 49% gain over the last five years.

Some of these shifts reflect broader market moods, especially how investors view risk and long-term growth for finance and data companies. With so many moving parts, the stock’s current valuation is a hot topic. According to our checklist, S&P Global is undervalued in just 1 out of 6 major valuation metrics, earning itself a value score of 1. In the next section, I will break down what that really means, walking through the main ways analysts value a stock like S&P Global, and why the best measure of true value might actually come at the end of this article.

S&P Global scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: S&P Global Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model provides a different lens on stock valuation. Instead of only looking at profits or dividends, this approach measures how much value a company creates above the minimum return investors demand, known as the cost of equity. Put simply, it examines whether investing in S&P Global’s business generates profits that beat the company’s own cost for using investor funds.

For S&P Global, the numbers are telling. The estimated Book Value stands at $109.37 per share, while analysts forecast a stable Earnings Per Share (EPS) of $18.38, based on a range of future estimates. That compares to a Cost of Equity of $9.30 per share, which means each share is expected to generate an "Excess Return" of $9.07. The firm’s average Return on Equity comes in at a strong 16.33%, and the Stable Book Value is projected to climb to $112.55 per share over time.

Pulling these figures together, the model estimates S&P Global’s intrinsic value per share at $287.43. Compared to the current market price, the discount implied is -68%, indicating the stock is significantly overvalued using this approach.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for S&P Global.

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests S&P Global may be overvalued by 68.0%. Find undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Approach 2: S&P Global Price vs Earnings

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a widely used tool for valuing profitable companies like S&P Global because it tells us how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of earnings. This makes it especially relevant for mature businesses with steady profits.

What the market considers a "normal" or “fair” PE ratio depends a lot on the company’s future earnings potential and the perceived risks. Companies with stronger growth prospects or lower risks often command higher PE ratios, while lower growth or riskier businesses tend to see lower multiples.

S&P Global currently trades at a PE ratio of 36.8x, placing it well above the Capital Markets industry average of 25.7x and its immediate peer group average of 31.6x. Simply Wall St’s proprietary “Fair Ratio” for S&P Global comes in at 20.2x. This figure is based on a detailed assessment of the company’s earnings growth, profit margins, market cap and risks specific to its industry.

The Fair Ratio offers a more complete picture than just using straight industry or peer averages because it adapts to the company’s unique traits and risk profile. Comparing S&P Global’s current PE to its Fair Ratio suggests the stock is quite a bit more expensive than its fundamentals alone might justify.

Result: OVERVALUED

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let's introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is simply your own financial story for a company like S&P Global, connecting your assumptions about future revenue, margins, and growth with your view of fair value. This powerful tool is already available on Simply Wall St’s Community page, where millions of investors share and compare their perspectives. Narratives link a company’s evolving story directly to a dynamic fair value estimate, so you can instantly see how new news or earnings updates may affect your outlook and whether the share price stacks up. data.title: Assessing USA Rare Earth After 145% Surge and Biden Administration Critical Minerals Push
data.description:
If you’ve got your eye on USA Rare Earth, you’re probably wondering whether now is the right time to act, or if the sizzle in the recent stock chart is just the start of something bigger. It’s hard to ignore a stock that’s up 24.6% over the past week and has risen an impressive 145.1% over the last month. Looking further back, USA Rare Earth has delivered a 213.0% return over the past year, with its shares now closing at $33.68. Such rapid gains can leave investors buzzing with excitement, but also questioning what’s really driving all this momentum and what the risk-reward equation looks like from here.

One catalyst behind this surge appears to be renewed market interest in critical minerals and supply chain resilience, themes that have pushed rare earth suppliers into the spotlight. Investors have begun reassessing potential growth for companies well-positioned in this space, with optimism lifting the sector. Still, every rally also raises fresh questions about the stock’s valuation. A look at USA Rare Earth’s valuation score, which comes in at 2 out of a possible 6 checks for undervaluation, suggests there’s more to investigate before jumping in.

So, where does that leave would-be investors? It's time to dig deeper into what the numbers say about USA Rare Earth’s value, and to consider not just standard valuation measures, but an even smarter approach that could give you the edge. Let’s break it all down.

USA Rare Earth scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: USA Rare Earth Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company’s value by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s dollars. This method allows investors to look beyond short-term results and measure intrinsic value based on long-term business potential.

For USA Rare Earth, recent financials show a free cash outflow of $31.5 Million; however, analysts foresee a dramatic turnaround. By 2029, analyst projections point to positive free cash flow of $122.6 Million. Further forecasts from Simply Wall St extend to 2035. Over the next ten years, free cash flow growth is expected to accelerate, reflecting optimism about the company’s ability to scale production and capture demand for critical minerals.

Based on this two-stage DCF model, USA Rare Earth’s estimated intrinsic value is $79.40 per share, using all cash flows in US dollars. With the current share price at $33.68, the DCF implies the stock is trading at a 57.6% discount to its intrinsic value. This signals substantial potential based on long-term cash generation prospects.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for USA Rare Earth.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests USA Rare Earth is undervalued by 57.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover more undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: USA Rare Earth Price vs Book

For companies in capital-intensive sectors like metals and mining, the Price-to-Book (PB) ratio is a particularly relevant metric. This valuation multiple compares a company's market value to its net assets, making it especially useful for firms where tangible assets dominate the balance sheet. Investors often prefer PB ratios in cases where profits are currently negative, but asset backing is strong or improving.

Growth expectations and risk both have a major effect on what is considered a reasonable PB ratio. Higher growth companies or those seen as safer typically command higher PB multiples, while riskier or slower-growing firms tend to trade at lower ones. Industry averages give us a sense of the general market consensus. Currently, the average PB ratio in the Metals and Mining sector stands at 2.43x, while the peer group for USA Rare Earth averages 27.12x.

USA Rare Earth’s PB ratio, however, is -24.45x, reflecting its negative book value. While industry averages and peer comparisons are informative, Simply Wall St's proprietary "Fair Ratio" digs deeper. This tailored multiple is calculated by blending factors like the company’s expected growth, risk profile, profit margins, industry sector, and even its size. By accounting for all these variables, the Fair Ratio aims to set a more precise benchmark than blunt industry averages.

In context, the Fair Ratio for USA Rare Earth is not materially different from its actual PB ratio, suggesting that the current share price is reasonable relative to what fundamentals and prospects justify. It appears investors are pricing in the company’s specific challenges and future potential.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

PB ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your USA Rare Earth Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let’s introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is a clear story that you create about a company, your take on its future, supported by numbers like fair value, potential revenue, earnings, and margins. Instead of relying only on standard metrics, Narratives help you link the business’s unique journey to a logical financial forecast and a custom estimate of what the company is really worth.

On Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives make sophisticated investing easy and accessible. Millions of investors use these tools to compare their own view of fair value with the latest share prices, helping decide when to buy or sell. In addition, Narratives are always relevant, thanks to instant updates as soon as new news or earnings come in.

For example, it is common for one investor’s Narrative for USA Rare Earth to put its fair value at over $90 per share, while another believes it is less than $20, each based on different assumptions about growth or risks. Narratives give you the power to see, test, and trust your own story, shaping smarter decisions in a changing market.
